title: Joseph Gordon-Levitt Talks Hollywood and an Ethical Future With AI
text:

As one of Hollywood’s earliest and most passionate supporters of
technology
-based community building, actor Joseph Gordon-Levitt has some choice words for those benefiting from a system that he says has veered wildly off course.
“The tech industry has become a bunch of Lex Luthors,” Gordon-Levitt said (invoking
Superman’s
notorious evil nemesis) on Jan. 25 as part of
The Hollywood Reporter
and
Autodesk
‘s
tech-focused panel event, held in partnership with Berggruen Institute, at the
Sundance
Film Festival in Park City, Utah.
Related Stories
TV
Colbert and Letterman Launch Pieces of 'Late Show' Set Off Ed Sullivan Theater Onto CBS Logo Ahead of Show's Final Week
Music
Jack Antonoff Lambasts AI Music Creators as "Godless Whores"
“In 2010, we all thought [those in the] industry were heroes — the smartest, brightest minds who were going to fix this messed-up world with their incredible technology … but what came of all those posts about the Arab Spring? What came of all those posts about Occupy Wall Street? They weren’t productive because they took place on platforms that weren’t designed for human connection.”
He said platforms like Facebook and X (formerly Twitter) were designed to hook, keep and serve you ads.
“Today,
AI
companies are saying  ‘Don’t put us in the same bucket as these social-media companies. AI is just here to help!’ And now, 100 percent of the value of [new AI tools] is going into four or five companies.”
Gordon-Levitt, who broke out as a child star in such films as 1992’s
A River Runs Through It
, directed by Robert Redford, and the hit NBC comedy
Third Rock From the Sun,
launched his online collaborative media platform HITRECORD at Sundance 16 years ago and has since become an impassioned voice for ethical and safe uses of technology.
He is a co-founder alongside numerous industry leaders (including Daniel Kwan and Janet Yang, also featured onstage at the event) of the recentl -formed Hollywood advocacy group Creators Coalition on AI.
One of the group’s many concerns, said Gordon-Levitt, is to reframe Hollywood artists’ understandings of how this technology threatens both their livelihoods and identities.
“It’s called ‘artificial intelligence,’ but let’s call it ‘collective,’” he said, citing that AI products don’t artificially generate anything; rather, they break humanity down into pieces of data. “And it doesn’t work without all that human input … [so] we need to build a system for ongoing compensation.”
Watch the full conversation above, in which Gordon-Levitt also addresses dangers posed to children by what he calls AI’s “predatory addictive algorithms” and what is keeping him hopeful for the future.
